0.name: Prof. Candice
0.text: Alright, now that the assessment is done, let's move on to our discussions!

1.name: Prof. Candice
1.text: I have another demonstration lined up for you guys later. But anyway...

2.name: Prof. Candice
2.text: Last week, we learned about acceleration.

3.name: Prof. Candice
3.text: Today, I'll be teaching you about the relationship between force and acceleration

4.name: Prof. Candice
4.text: I'm sure you guys have heard of that infamous formula: F = ma.

5.name: Prof. Candice
5.text: Well, it falls under Newton's Second Law of Motion which states that:

6.text: "The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it but inversely proportional to its mass."

7.name: Prof. Candice
7.text: Let's proceed to our first example!

8.name: Prof. Candice
8.text: If anyone in here can recall, the formula for it is...?

9.name: Perry
9.text: F[font size=12]net[/font] = ma, where F[font size=12]net[/font] is the net force measured in Newtons N.

9.1.name: Perry
9.1.text: N is equal to [pulse](kg m/s^2 )[/pulse], m is mass in (kg), and a is the acceleration [pulse](m/s^2)[/pulse].

11.name: Prof. Candice
11.text: For our first question: Estimate the net force needed to accelerate a 1000kg car at ½ g.

13.name: Prof. Candice
13.text: To solve this problem, we will use the formula: F[font size=12]net[/font] ​= ma.

14.name: Prof. Candice
14.text: First, we substitute their values with the given: F = (1000kg)(½g).

15.name: Prof. Candice
15.text: Here, we see that m = 1000kg of the car is multiplied by ½ of g or gravity.

16.name: Prof. Candice
16.text: This g stands for acceleration due to gravity, and we know that g is equal to 9.8m/s^2.

17.name: Prof. Candice
17.text: With that in mind, this now gives us: F = (1000kg)(4.9m/s^2), which is equal to...?

19.1.name: Prof. Candice
19.1.text: Yes, that is correct, the answer is 5000 N.

19.2.name: Prof. Candice
19.2.text: No Colin, that is wrong, the correct answer is 5000 N.

20.name: Prof. Candice
20.text: The 4.9m/s^2 is a given from the 9.8m/s^2 is multiplied by our problem given ½ g.

21.name: Prof. Candice
21.text: We changed the unit into N or Newtons due to our statement earlier that N = kg m/s^2.

22.name: Prof. Candice
22.text: We will now proceed to our second example.

23.name: Prof. Candice
23.text: A worker with spikes on his shoes pulls with a constant horizontal force of...

24.name: Prof. Candice
24.text: 20N on a box with mass of 40kg on the flat [pulse]frictionless[/pulse] surface of a frozen lake.

25.name: Prof. Candice
25.text: Class, note here that “frictionless” means that the ice doesn't have any force to oppose the motion.

26.name: Prof. Candice
26.text: Since there is only one horizontal component of force, we will change our formula from: F[font size=12]net[/font] ​= ma to a[font size=12]x[/font] ​= F[font size=12]x[/font]/m

27.name: Prof. Candice
27.text: After substitution, it becomes: a[font size=12]x[/font] = 20N/40kg

30.1.name: Prof. Candice
30.1.text: No, the answer is 0.5m/s^3. Do you not have your calculator out? Anyway...

30.2.name: Prof. Candice
30.2.text: Yes, that is right. The answer is 0.5m/s^3. Good job!

31.name: Prof. Candice
31.text: Let's now proceed to our demonstration!

32.name: Prof. Candice
32.text: Come on up, Colin. Time to shine!

34.name: Prof. Candice
34.text: Try shoving them each at full force.
